concerns.text: SC-001 defines success as 'pass@1 ≥ 2pp higher OR Wilcoxon p < 0.05'. Using the statistical test result (computed from the same data) as a success criterion creates circular validation. The p-value is not an independent validation target—it is derived from the pass@1 measurements themselves.

SC-002 has the same circularity: 'median time ≤ 90% OR Wilcoxon p < 0.05'. The p-value is computed from the latency measurements, so using it as an alternative success criterion is tautological. A claim that 'the test shows significance' cannot validate the test's own input data.

Constitution Principle VII states 'results are considered valid only if the p-value is < 0.05'. This makes statistical significance a validity gate rather than a reporting metric. Validity should depend on experimental design quality, not on whether a particular threshold is crossed.

FR-005 specifies Bonferroni correction for two hypotheses. However, SC-001 and SC-002 use an OR relationship between effect size and significance, which undermines the correction's purpose. The analysis plan should specify whether effect size thresholds OR significance (not both) determine success.

SC-003 measures token reduction (≤95% for ≥80% of problems). This is empirically testable but should clarify whether this is a prerequisite for the accuracy/latency analysis or a separate success criterion. Currently it's unclear if token reduction must be achieved before other metrics are evaluated.
